---
title: "The Fan Growth Engine"
subtitle: "A data-driven CRM strategy for improving fan retention, lifecycle engagement, and long-term supporter value."
image: ../images/logo.png
format: html
page-layout: article
---

## Overview

The Fan Growth Engine was a strategy and analytics project focused on a major commercial question in sport: how do you turn one-off attendance into long-term fan relationships? The project examined the Football Association of Ireland’s fan development challenge and identified a structural retention problem hidden beneath strong public interest and major attendance moments.

Rather than treating fan growth as a simple marketing issue, the project approached it as a CRM and lifecycle design problem. The central argument was that the FAI already had interest, attention, and audience momentum, but lacked the data infrastructure and retention architecture required to convert those assets into sustained fan value.

## Problem / Context

The project identified three connected weaknesses. First, attendance was heavily parent-driven at younger ages, meaning support was often transactional rather than independently loyal. Second, there was a sharp drop-off around age 18, when young supporters disappeared from organisational data systems. Third, grassroots and ticketing data were siloed, preventing effective segmentation and personalised communication.

Taken together, these issues meant the organisation was repeatedly reacquiring fans it should have been retaining. That is commercially inefficient and strategically risky, especially during a period when women’s football in Ireland had strong momentum and visibility.

## Approach / Methodology

The project used a sequence of frameworks to move from diagnosis to strategic design. These included PESTEL, SWOT, TOWS, Value Proposition Canvas, Customer Journey analysis, and Business Model Canvas. Rather than using frameworks as isolated academic labels, the project linked them together so that each one led to a clear strategic decision.

The final strategy centred on lifecycle segmentation across four groups: Kids, Teens, Students, and Adults. The student segment was identified as the critical intervention point because it represented the highest churn risk and one of the strongest long-term value opportunities.

## What I did

- Contributed to analysing fan-retention and CRM issues within the FAI context.
- Worked with strategic frameworks including PESTEL, SWOT, TOWS, Customer Journey, and Business Model Canvas.
- Helped shape a lifecycle segmentation strategy across Kids, Teens, Students, and Adults.
- Explored the role of data architecture, CRM workflows, and retention triggers in fan development.
- Connected strategic recommendations to commercial logic including CAC, LTV, and revenue growth thinking.

## Results / Outcomes

Two standout initiatives helped make the strategy practical. The first was the Student ID Scheme and Lucky 8 Draw, which combined affordability, CRM data capture, and repeat-attendance incentives into one mechanism. The second was the Players Teach Programme, which linked community engagement with future conversion by turning grassroots contact into a consent-based fan pipeline.

What made these initiatives strong was that they were not random campaign ideas. They were designed to solve a specific structural problem: the loss of fan relationships during life-stage transition.

## Why it matters

This project was important because it showed how business analytics can be applied beyond dashboards and predictive models. Here, the value came from diagnosing a systems problem, structuring evidence, segmenting users, and designing a commercially credible solution.

It also highlighted the power of CRM thinking. A supporter does not become loyal by accident; retention usually depends on timing, relevance, incentives, and the organisation’s ability to act on its own data.

## What I learned

This project strengthened my ability to connect analytics with strategy. I learned how segmentation, lifecycle logic, and commercial metrics such as customer acquisition cost and lifetime value can shape better decisions even when the output is not a machine-learning model.

It also taught me that data problems are often organisational problems in disguise. If systems are fragmented and relationships are not tracked properly, growth becomes harder no matter how much public interest already exists.

## Tools / Skills

- CRM strategy
- Customer lifecycle design
- Segmentation
- Business model thinking
- SWOT / TOWS
- Value Proposition Canvas
- Customer Journey Mapping
- Commercial analytics
- Sports business strategy

## Takeaway

This project helped me show that I can apply analytical thinking not only to technical modelling, but also to strategic growth problems where data, customer behaviour, and commercial design are closely connected.
